Hi, this is Rick Warner. I'm here with a very special guest, David Deutsch. David, thanks for coming. Thanks, Rick. It's great to be here. I seem to remember that you used to work for a highly respected advertising agency in New York, Ogilvy & Mather. Is that right? I was a copywriter at Ogilvy & Mather. Right. What kind of stuff did you work on? Accounts like Maxwell House Coffee, American Express, Merrill Lynch. I happened to know that at some point you used to work with stuff that was going on in the Caribbean like vacation stuff, right? Well, I did a guidebook to the Caribbean. It was one of the things that I did while I was there. Did you get to do some research then? Yeah, we got to go down there. Got to go down to Bermuda. What sort of led you in the direction of what you're doing now? Well, I got into writing direct mail. And as you know, in direct mail and direct response, unlike in advertising, the results are very measurable. And so since I was being measured and being evaluated according to my results, indeed even being paid according to the results, I suddenly became very results oriented and looked for ways to get better results, come up with ideas that would lead to better results. So you're constantly having to come up with new ideas. Right. Did that get to be a lot of pressure at certain points? Oh, sure. I think one of the things that really distinguishes the people who are best at direct response, writing, is not so much the quality of their writing as the quality of their ideas. So it was like really important to be able to come up with new ideas or there's no bread on the table, right? Exactly. In the process of going through this business of having to come up with new ideas all the time, what led you to this particular thinking inside the box? Well, I think what led me to the search for a method or a system to better come up with ideas was just the frustration of the hit and miss of coming up with ideas and not knowing when you're going to come up with a good idea, where it was going to come from, waiting for it to sort of come out of nowhere. When you discovered this, were you just like pleased as punch? Oh, yeah. I was very, very happy to finally have a really systematic way to go about searching for ideas. So it must have been a big relief. Oh, sure. Did you at that point sort of share this with some other people to see how they did with it? No, I kept it to myself. So only now we're beginning to find out. Only now do we find out. The value of what you discovered. Why do I need this system? Well, I think, first of all, it's really important to be creative in all aspects of your life. I think to some extent the quality of your life, not just the quality of your work, is determined by the quality of the ideas that you're capable of coming up with. Whether that's what to cook for dinner or what to write in a direct mail package. I think also most people tend to get very set in their ways when it comes to thinking. They have certain ruts that they think in. And so they tend to come up with the same type of ideas. They get stuck in the same place over and over. And what this system does is enable you to systematically go about looking at things in different ways. I call it looking in boxes where you're able to generate ideas in different ways. It's exciting to think that there's something out there that can help you solve a problem in a very systematic way. So how do I decide which problems would be appropriate to use with this system? Well, I think really, I hate to say it's good for any problem, but it really is good for any problem. And I think the important thing is to just try it, see what problems it works with. Because it may not work with everything or it could work with everything. I think it could work with everything. Because a lot of your examples here have to do with like running a business, but there are also some really nice examples in there about how to deal with your daughter who's starting to date. Right. So I mean that's a pretty diverse bunch of problems there. Why do you call this a system? Well, it's called a system because it's a systematic way of approaching creativity. I think for most people, creativity coming up with ideas is a very hit and miss kind of a thing. They don't really have a systematic way of looking for ideas. You know, if you've ever seen a search teamwork for a plane crash, the army, when they're searching for something really important, they'll divide it up into grids and they'll very systematically search very large areas, but they'll do it in a systematic way to make sure that every inch is covered. And that's really what this does is it makes sure that you search every inch of where the most leverage is for coming up with creative ideas, rather than thinking outside the box, which just means you have this huge universe to look in and an idea can be anywhere, and you don't even know where to start looking. That's interesting because there's a lot of people, including myself, who have a set way of thinking about being creative and that phrase always comes up, think outside the box, think outside the box. Well, thinking outside the box could produce a result, but it also could just leave you dead in the water. Right. And I think there's, it's kind of a double-edged sword. There's a good side and a bad side to thinking outside the box. It's a nice metaphor for going beyond your traditional way of thinking. It also kind of leaves you hanging in this vast universe of not even knowing where to start. Or how to implement, which this system does. A couple of different things that I was interested in when I read the book, and they seem like really key elements. One of the things that I picked out where you talk about the word impossible. First of all, I think all of us has had enough experience and read about enough things to realize nothing is really impossible. So many seemingly impossible things have really been accomplished. But I think as soon as you, as soon as you cut off a line of thought by saying it's impossible, you cut off a lot of ideas that while they may actually be impossible, could lead to ideas that are possible. Something else, right? Something better. Right. And it would be possible if only we did this, if only we modified it in this way. So if I use the word impossible, that just stops me dead in my tracks. Right. So you just need to suspend that for a while. I guess the other one that I wanted to ask about was, which I thought was very useful, had to do with defining the problem. It impressed me because it means that you have to articulate something and try to be accurate about it. Well, I think so many people never take that really important step of knowing exactly what the problem is that they're trying to solve. The problem is something very narrow and they try to solve that and not see therefore that they could solve the problem in another way. But they've defined the problem as being X and they're only trying to solve X. Let me give you an example. Okay. Let's say, a very simple example is of store. And they define the problem as how can we get more customers? And so they put all their efforts into trying to get more customers when they there may be much more potential in getting the customers that they have to spend more money. And so they may not have to get more customers. They just need to concentrate on getting the customers they have to spend more money. It's two ways of accomplishing more profitability. But if you define the problem as how do we get more customers instead of how do we get more profitability? You're leaving a whole area untapped. I'd like to know from you because you're the expert with this system, right? Do you suppose this system of thinking inside the box will be with my biggest problem? Being organized. I have a degree of organization in the things that I do. It's kind of like when you walk into your basement and you see your work table and it's just a mess. But you know where everything is. Controlled chaos. Yes. Right. Well, I think certainly it could help. Your answer is yes. You know, one of the questions you start with defining the problem. You know, is the problem that you don't know how to be organized or that you're unwilling to do what you know you need to do to be organized? I think that if I understood better how to be organized, I wouldn't resist it so much. Well, again, I think the problem that you need to define the problem. And if the problem is that you don't know where to find things, then that represents different kinds of solutions than you want to be more. I don't think you want to be more organized. I think you want to be able to find things. Yes. No question about it. Right. Being able to find things might mean you never were organized. Maybe you hire someone to kind of look out for your stuff. But see already that's like a new way to think about it. Right. I think then the next step is obviously using the system, using the boxes to find more creative, better ideas for being organized. And as you go through, you know, all the boxes, you may find things like hiring an assistant, doing this, doing that, even just coming up with 50 ideas really quickly may lead you to some ways that you could be more organized, that you could find things better. You know, I'm interested in doing this. I'm not, you know, it's not like my life's not over yet. There's always something to organize. So anyway, I was going to ask you about there. There's a number of different things that sort of come into play in terms of thinking inside the box and relax. It is one of them getting sleep, you know, being fresh and having fun. It seems like they're all sort of connected in a way. Yeah, I think that's a real good insight. I think relaxing, getting more sleep, playful are all things that open one up to ideas and creativity. If you're tense, if you're serious, you tend to kind of block yourself off from having ideas. Humor is nothing more than making unexpected associations. So it's a great way to open yourself up to creativity, loosen up your creativity muscles as it were. So would it be a good idea to do a little stand-up comedy before you start your think-inside-the-box section? Yeah, definitely. A lot of people start their creativity sessions by joking around, making jokes, just being funny, laughing, then leading into coming up with really off-the-wall funny ideas and finding that from those off-the-wall funny ideas something kind of emerges that's a seriously good idea. I think that's great because it gives you a way of confronting something and not leaving it just sort of floating around in mid-air and having it nag you all the time. You know, there is a certain nice kind of confidence that comes from knowing that you've explored every angle to a problem. You know, when you're done doing that, when you get to the last box and you've looked in it, you can kind of just sort of stop and say, okay, I've looked at this from every angle. I've come up with all the ideas that I can. Whereas if you don't have a systematic way of doing it, you're always sort of feeling, if I put another hour into this, I could come up with something better. There's a page that has all the boxes on it. So going back to the systematic idea, do you pick and choose? Do you go through all the boxes at once? I'm just really trying to find out what's the best approach to working with these boxes. One thing is to just go quickly through all the boxes and see, you know, which one seemed to work for that problem. But I think after a while you start to find that there are certain boxes that work better for certain kinds of problems. Like if I'm writing a headline, I find that there are certain boxes that just seem to work better for headlines. Like the whole idea of reversing something, doing the opposite. So it's good for a headline. It's always good to see what's the opposite kind of a headline that I can do where it just flips it around. So I think you'll find for yourself, maybe certain boxes, you know, trigger areas of creativity that aren't usually triggered in you, so you'll like those boxes and tend to use those. Don't feel obliged to go through each box in great detail. You know, as I say, you know, read through one box, and nothing hits me. Just go to the next one. Don't get bogged down. When you're working with any kind of system, there are those people who just really insist on getting everything perfect the way you laid it out. Then there are those people that kind of like show me. They're looking for the system to prove something to them. And the nice thing that you said to me was just take a box a day. Think about how that box affects that problem. Yeah, I think that's a good idea. Take one box every day, go through it, go through the day, and see things in relation to that box. You know, maybe it's, you know, going to keep coming back to doing the opposite because that's a fairly obvious one. You know, how would it be if I did this differently? How would I be if it did the opposite? After you've worked with this for a while, do you get to a point where you just instinctively know which box fits with the thing that you're trying to solve? Yeah, I think for some people, they know instinctively which box may work for the problem they're trying to solve. I think some people are always going to be the kind of people that want to go through each box and just see if there's something to be seen by looking at the problem in this box. And it happens after a while, too, is that this kind of thinking becomes intuitive. Your mind just kind of on its own sees things in the way that the boxes make you see things. Your mind itself starts asking the questions in the boxes. And I think that's what creative geniuses do naturally. They were born being able to look at things in a different way. I like your idea about 50 ideas. Do you think that's a really good jumping off point in terms of getting started with this thing? Well, I think that's something that anyone can do instantly. The next time you have a problem and need solutions, need ideas, just force yourself to write down 50 of them. Don't worry about whether they're good or they're bad or they're wrong or they'll work or they won't work. Just come up with 50 of them as fast as you can. Just make yourself do it. Yeah, I've done that before. It's fabulous. It's just a really, really great way to sort of energize your mind and open it. My other question has to do with persistence. How do you rate persistence in the mix of everything? Well, I think persistence is just absolutely crucial. Sometimes it seems like the only difference between normal people and geniuses is just that geniuses persist longer. That story of Edison and inventing the light bulb and going through 5,000 different elements before he found what would work in a light bulb. How many people have the persistence to do that? And certainly in coming up with ideas. So many people give up right before they have a great idea. When I've come up with a good idea, it's kind of scared me. It was at the point where I was ready to give up, persisted a little more, and I came up with the idea that was needed. That's really great. You write down those 50 ideas a lot of times. The number 49 and 50 are going to be the good ones. Because you've gotten all the junk out of your system. You've gotten your mind warmed up by that time. And also the system really helps you to be persistent because if you didn't have a system, you'd just give up at that certain point. You wouldn't be in a place to go. That's a very good point. It's different to try to be persistent when you're sitting there going, think outside the box. I've got to think outside the box. I've got to come up with a great idea. I've got to think outside the box. Or I'm going to be dead. There's a difference between that and sitting there and saying, okay, box number three, here I go. Box number four, it gives you a plan. David, there's a lot of people out there very busy. Is there some quick start method you can use with this system? Well, I think Rick, the best way to start is just to take one box and work with it for a day or two or whatever. Just go through the day. Any problems that come up, anything you encounter, just try to look at it through the questions in that box. If it's the first box, look at it in terms of adding a new dimension to it somehow. So I don't have to stop everything that I'm doing and just immerse myself in this. Right. You can certainly take one box. Any one box can have just, I need to say, life changing implications, but can certainly lead to an idea that could change your life or change your business. How long in your experience, how long does it take before you feel like this system is worth its weight in gold? Well, I think the first time it helps you come up with an idea that you wouldn't have come up with ordinarily, you certainly feel that way. So actually, that's your proof. I think the system will prove itself fairly quickly to you. Do you find that when you're talking to people and talking about thinking inside the box, that that's one of their primary concerns is proof? I think a lot of people respond to this pretty intuitively in terms of it being a good idea, in terms of, yeah, I've kind of always known there must be a way to think systematically and be creative systematically. So they can sort of see the value in it before they've done it. Everybody out there wants to know how do you go about solving a problem? Well, I think the first thing, the most important thing is just to kind of clear your mind of everything else. It's what Maxwell Maltz who invented psychocybernetics called clearing the calculator. It's like hitting the clear button on the calculator. You get everything else out of your mind. As they say in Zen, you can't fill the tea cup when it's already full. And when your mind is empty of other things, other concerns, other worries, it's just so much more receptive to new ideas. It's kind of like your mind becomes a vacuum, nature abhors a vacuum, and the ideas just come streaming in. I don't know if you've ever had the experience of having ideas come to you when you're just not thinking about anything. You're not trying to think of the idea they just come to you. That's the way I start. I don't even use the system first. I just want to see what comes to me without it. I want to see what comes to me without even doing too much homework or background work on the problem. Because when you start to know too much, you start to have preconceived notions about things. You start to limit yourself. Let's say I get an assignment from a client. I'm going to work on this newsletter. Right away sometimes I'll start coming up with ideas for it. Before I know too much about the author, too much about what the client thinks it should be like, I'll just start writing down ideas. And some of those are the ideas. Better than the ideas I'll come up with once I've read everything that I'm supposed to read about it. So you can't walk into this worried. Right. And then after I've come up with ideas doing that, then I'll apply the boxes. Then I'll go through it systematically to come up with more ideas. The way you start has to do more with your instinct and then you use the system to flesh it out. No. I think it's just two different ways of going about it. And you know, I would emphasize that this is what works for me, for whatever, because of my peculiarities of my mind. Someone else might work better to start with the system. Box number 12. Exactly. Or box number 12. Did we get stuck for an idea? Not since I've started doing this, because you always just go through the boxes systematically and start looking at it. It's not like you're not going to come up with anything, right? Exactly. So what's the difference between a good idea and a great idea? Do you just know that? That's a good question. I think it's something that you largely know intuitively. It just rings with a greatness, with an applicability. I think a great idea is an obvious idea. It's an idea that when you hear it, you go, my God, that's so obvious. Why did it take so long to think about it? Or this is it. Or this is it, right? It's not like you have to go looking around anymore. You know it's a great idea. But there's also a trap in that. There's a trap in getting to an idea, thinking it's a great idea, and stopping there, when either maybe it's not such a great idea, or maybe there are greater ideas to be had. And it's a very great temptation. When you're a writer, for instance, as I am, through a great temptation to say, okay, this is the idea I'm going to start writing now and get this thing over with. But, you know, often I find... It's even worse to discover that it's not such a great idea, if you've done all that work. Exactly. How's this program going to make me a lot of money? Well, that's certainly one way to use this program. Ways of making money are just ideas. You know, having a better idea of how to do something. How to find an idea of how to find a need that people have, and an idea for a profitable way to fill that need. That's what making money is. You're selling ideas, really. Look at the pet rock. The pet rock was a pure idea. Somebody took a rock, put it in a box, labeled it a pet rock, put instruction booklet with it, and made money with it. Made money with the idea. The money actually is a consequence of something. So it's not like just having a great idea, but it's having a system like, think inside the box is like the foundation. I mean, anybody can have a great idea, but think inside the box encourages you to implement those ideas so that it becomes something three-dimensional rather than just, I had a great idea. So what? I think you've pointed out something interesting, that there's two different things. One is having an idea, and the other thing is what you do with the idea. And I think the system can help you on both levels. It can help you come up with great ideas. But you're absolutely right. If you don't implement it, if you don't do anything with it, it doesn't matter how great the idea is. And so you need ideas for how to implement that idea. You need ideas for what to do with it. I mean, the guy that came up with the pet rock, that didn't make him any money just to come up with it. He had to go out and he had to sell it to gift stores. He had to get on the radio and find a way to get publicity. All that stuff, yeah. Can this idea be used by a group of people? I think using it in a group even enhances it because you get all the different perspectives of people in that group looking at it with the different questions in the boxes. You know, everyone's got a different way of looking. What is adding a dimension mean to you? It's different from what adding a dimension means to me. So use that group dynamic and build off that. The idea that I'll come up with, you'll think of something based on that idea. You'll improve on it. The interesting thing to me is that it appeals not just to people who are in a creative profession or in a creative group. This could apply to people in a church group. It could apply to people who are a parents group. It could apply to lots of different things that really don't have anything to do with business. Yes, I think that's very true. Maybe something we didn't emphasize enough when we talked about it in the beginning. You can be creative in any area of your life. You need ideas in any area of your life for better ways to do things, for better ways to get along with your spouse, for better ways to raise your kids, for better ideas for how to work out the logistics of getting your kids to school and a thousand things. And in a way that makes a nice practice for the system to use it on a day-to-day basis like that. Then when you need a good idea at work, you're kind of familiar with the system. Your brain is kind of open. More available than it would be. Can we just talk a second about how different types of people might be able to use this program? Sure. How might, say for instance, a salesperson use the program? Well, I think good salesmanship is largely about having good ideas for how to sell something. Good ideas for how to present your product. Good ideas for how to get through to customers. What's a creative way to get that person on the phone that won't take your calls? What's a creative way to convince someone to buy the product? What about a manager? Well, I think it's great for being a manager. So often we relate to people in the same way time after time. You know, we all have our habitual ways of relating to people. And sometimes interpersonal problems or managerial problems are that this person is not responding to the way we habitually relate to people and manage people. Again, this system helps you get out of your rut and say, OK, what would happen if I did the opposite? Maybe this person doesn't need hand-holding. Maybe this person needs the opposite. Maybe he needs just to be let go and ignored by me. Or more explanation. Yeah. I mean, any number of things. It just gives you different things to try, different ideas. How about someone who wants to move up the corporate ladder? I think the people that are the most valuable to a company are the people that have the best ideas. You know, you can talk about hard-working. You can talk about being good at presentations. You can talk about a lot of things that people can do. But the people of value in most companies are the people that come up with the ideas. Entrepreneur? Entrepreneur. All these things apply. An entrepreneur has to be a salesman. An entrepreneur has to be a good manager. An entrepreneur has to come up with the ideas that's going to make the company money. PR, advertising. He's got the whole load. Right. And you know, what is an entrepreneur after all but a person with an idea? Hopefully a lot of ideas. I personally have bought a lot of self-improvement products and learned a lot of good information. But then in a couple of weeks, I just forget it. How can I make sure that doesn't happen with this? Well, I think the most important thing is that you apply the material as quickly as possible and you start using it. Because that's what builds connections in the web of your mind that's your memory. Makes things stick to that web. Just make using it a habit. I can promise you, you'll wonder how you ever lived without it. I don't mean to sound like a salesperson for it, but it really is incredible when you start systematically looking for ideas. I mean, there are people who go out and get something and say, that's a nice idea. It's not the fault of the material, so to speak. It's because I didn't apply it. Well, I think we're all guilty of that. Before I started using this system, there are a lot of systems that I came across in terms of being more creative that I thought when I found them. Hey, this is a great system. I need to do this. Not just for creativity, but for a lot of self-improvement type things. But unless you really start doing it, they don't stick with you. But this one obviously has. This one did stick with me because it was such a good system for coming up with ideas. I could see that right away and started using it right away and it started working right away. What's the best way to use your pocket idea generator? Well, the pocket idea generator lists all the different boxes nicely on a single page. It's just a handy way of having them all together where you can go through them quickly and see how they apply the situation to generating ideas. So this is something you can just carry around in your pocket. Exactly. Could you tell me how do you go about using these boxes? Well, let me give you some examples of ways that I've used them. Let's go through each of the boxes. Box number one is the idea of adding a new dimension to something. Adding sound, sight, smell, touch. And a lot of times when I'm writing copy, it reminds me that I need to add some sensory elements to it. You're talking about something that makes your lawn greener. So you say, imagine hearing your neighbors raving about your lawn. All of a sudden you've added an element of hearing to that. You've added a whole new dimension to it. Okay, so that was box number one. How would you work with box number two? Do the opposite. I recently did a sales letter for a company that was very typically does very hypey letters about themselves and their products. This letter was going to go for a very expensive seminar. And what I did was instead of doing their usual very hypey, very kind of shouting how wonderful they are, I did it very understated. I did the opposite. I did something that was very understated and very maybe you're not qualified to come to this seminar. Let's see. Let me tell you a little bit about it and we'll see if you have the qualifications to come. Wow. And it was a very different kind of approach and it was very much doing the opposite. Box number three, divide something. Well, I think that a good example of that is the whole system that we're discussing. It really divides creativity and divides idea generation up into 15 boxes. So then all you have to do is just go inside each of the 15 boxes. So it's kind of dividing and conquering by doing that. Box number four, take something out. Recently I was working as a creative director in an agency. I found that the writers were very frustrated because of the amount of time that they had to spend doing production type things, setting things up and laying things out. And I realized that what I had to do was simply extract that function and create a new function that did those types of things, that made corrections to work so that the writers didn't get bogged down in that. And I think that's a good example of removing something, of taking something out. In order for something to work. Right. Box number five, bring together. Sometimes when I'm writing or trying to come up with ideas for headlines or titles, I'll think, what is something that people know that I can use? That I can make a twist to? That I can combine it with what I'm trying to do? A friend of mine, Joe Vitale, had written a book on P.T. Barnum and his marketing secrets and what you could learn about marketing and business from P.T. Barnum. It was a great book. And he asked me if I had any ideas for titles for it. And I thought about, well, what can you bring together? What do people know about P.T. Barnum? And I thought of the expression that he's known for, which he never said, by the way, there's a sucker born every minute. And I thought about how you could use that. And I came up with the idea of there's a customer born every minute since it's a book about marketing. And that's what Joe used for the title. That's great. Box number six, put one thing inside another. Great example of that is something that someone else did. They had a letter for international living, which was a newsletter on living outside the country, on traveling and things like that. And they had a letter that they had been mailing for it for a very long time and it was very successful. But the letter started to do less and less well as letters tend to do if you've been mailing them for a long time. And what they did was they put that letter inside a different envelope. And so you opened up the envelope and there was another envelope. And the message was, you know, I'm not sure if you got this letter. I just wanted to make sure I was sending it to you again. And there was that letter that hadn't been doing very well anymore. All of a sudden it had new life because they thought put one thing inside the other. So they put that envelope inside another envelope. It was the same letter. And it was the same letter. But suddenly they thought it was a different letter. Right. So that letter had more life to it, lived for a few more years. What about box number seven? Do it beforehand. Well, when I was doing some managing, the most important thing that I found was that preparation was really everything. That the more I did beforehand, the more I prepared for a meeting with someone, the better that that went. And that you just couldn't do that too much. You couldn't prepare too much. Doesn't that also have to do with confidence? Yes, because you know what you're going to say and you're prepared for the situation exactly. That's great. Okay. What about box number eight? Convert to benefit. Some of the work that I do is for high priced investment services. And you've got a very high price that you're having to sell. And that seems like a negative. And in terms of converting that into a benefit, you can actually make the high price a benefit by saying that, you know, it's proof of the quality and the exclusivity of the service. So the fact that it costs a lot for this service becomes a benefit. How about box number nine, switch gears? Well, I think that's a big thing. Writing. I think the best writing comes from going overboard. And that's one of the aspects of switching gears is going slower, going faster, or just going overboard, doing it even more than you think you should. And in writing, the best writing, I think, often comes from going overboard, being too enthusiastic, being too vivid, being too hypey. And then just cutting back from that to where you need to be. So I think it's actually a great writing technique. And I certainly used it as that. So going overboard is really going too far with something or taking something in a particular direction and just take it all the way. Right. Where you wouldn't tend to normally. What about box number 10, revise the rhythm? Well, I think a lot of times in marketing, the rhythm of something is so important. Is it done continuously? Is it done intermittently? One of the most interesting things going on right now is a good example of that is the use of auto responders where you go to a site, someone's trying to get you to something. You don't want to buy, but they say, hey, wait a minute, how about a little mini course? How about I send you a series of reports on this subject that you're interested in? And so they send you a report every week. And with that little email report, you also get a little pitch to buy what it is they're selling. So they've created a very different rhythm of the selling. Instead of the selling being a one-time hit or miss thing, it becomes a different rhythm. It becomes a weekly thing where you're getting this email every week. And that's made possible with something called auto responders that you've probably heard about that automatically send out emails. So it also means that you're becoming more involved with their products. Right. Kind of like a free sample. Box number 11, do it itself. Well, I think I go back to the management experience that I had recently where I kept finding that if you let people work out their own problems, create their own systems and procedures, they're usually much better and much more bought into than anything that management could create. So that was a big thing for me when I was a creative director, is let people do it themselves, let them figure it out, let them create the systems. Tell us about box number 12, change interactions or intensity. Well, I think again with copywriting, a lot of times I'll use that, I'll look at something and say, how can I make this more intense? Do I need to make it less intense? Just like I was talking about earlier, where a lot of times what this particular company did was very hypey and very intense. And when I made it less intense, it was a very different impression that it made on people. And it was a change of pace. You know, like in baseball, you know, when you throw a change of pace. The change up. Yup, change up and it messes up the batter because he's not expecting it. The same way with writing sometimes, you change the entity, you change the interaction and all of a sudden they're confused, they're not expecting that and you have a much better chance of selling what it is you're trying to sell when it's unexpected. I guess there's some kind of wake up factor there. Yup, well so much of marketing today and people are so overwhelmed with things, you have to wake them up first. I would think so. Okay, how about box number 13, add something? I think in copywriting, you know, one of the things about adding something is adding some kind of feedback to what you're doing. And sometimes in copy, the idea of adding feedback where you actually ask a question in the copy, you're soliciting feedback in a way from the reader. It's a very powerful thing and this kind of reminds me to do that, to be aware that there's a reader and to make it more of a conversation, to get feedback from him, to ask questions. One of the very powerful innovations that someone had many years ago was adding a box that said maybe to the order form. You know, yes, no and maybe. And of course maybe just meant they hadn't fully decided and would take it on the money back guarantee. So again, it's a way of getting the customer involved. Right, and they found that by adding the maybe box, it dramatically increased sales. And to me, that's a good example of getting feedback. People like to be, you know, ask their opinion. Some of the most powerful fundraising letters are surveys. That's a tremendously effective device because people like to be asked for their feedback. They're very honored to be asked their opinion on the environment. Please, you know, fill out this survey for us and answer these questions. And oh, by the way, would you like to donate some money to save the humpback whales or whatever? Okay, so box number 14, change the shape. Changing the shape has to do with adding, making it curved, making it straight, balancing, unbalancing. And one of the things that I think of a lot of times when I'm going through these boxes is in terms of balance. In terms of when you're writing, you're so locked into giving your point of view, telling what's wonderful about the product. That sometimes it really helps to give balance, to give the other point of view and say, well, you know, this product may not be for you. There are some things about it that may not make it right for everyone. People really like symmetry. They like that. There's one on this side, there's one on that side, and that's symmetrical, and it's perfect, and don't mess with it. Well, it's certainly another answer to this question is the whole idea of symmetry and asymmetrical and symmetrical. And a lot of times I'll look at a layout or something and just say it's just too symmetrical. It puts you to sleep because it's so well balanced. And you were talking earlier about needing to wake people up. Sometimes asymmetry, things being out of balance, wakes people up and gets their attention. What gets people's attention is when something is not balanced, when something is asymmetrical, not when it is symmetrical. Or especially if they're looking at something that should be curved and straight. Right. And they're like, no, no, that should be curved. So if you straighten it, you wake them up. Right. Or vice versa. Let's see, that was, oh, we're down to our last box. Box number 15, change the look. Well, one very successful promotion that I did a while ago was in a, instead of doing a traditional promotion called a magologue, which kind of looks like a magazine except it's trying to sell you a book or a newsletter, I made it look more like a newspaper and changed the look of it. And it was very successful because people were used to getting things in the mail that looked like magazines, but were actually selling books and newsletters. They weren't so used to getting things that looked like newspapers that were selling books and newsletters. So you just changed the look of it. And I changed the look of it. And that alone, I think, made it very successful. That's interesting, David. And that's box number 15, which is the end of this program. But first, I want to thank David Doyle for joining us. And I want to thank you folks for listening in. So go out there and have a good idea.
